speaker: interviewer
text: Carlos is not here, so--

speaker: interviewer
text: December 19, 1996, survivor Jenny Lerner. Interviewer, Betty Levinson City, Los Angeles, country, USA. Language, English.

speaker: interviewer
text: My name is Betty Levinson. Today's date is December 19, 1996. I'm conducting an interview with Jenny Lerner in Hollywood, California, USA. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Please say and spell your name.

speaker: interviewee
text: My name is Jenny Lerner, J-E-N-N-Y L-E-R-N-E-R.

speaker: interviewer
text: Have you ever used any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what were they?

speaker: interviewee
text: Eugenia Lewicka.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could your spell that, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: E-U-G-E-N-I-A L-E-W-I-C-K-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: And have you used any other names?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your birth date?

speaker: interviewee
text: August 21, 1912.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your age?

speaker: interviewee
text: Now? 84 and 3 months.

speaker: interviewee
text: And what city were you born in? Luck, was spelled L-U-C-K.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the country? The country was switching from one to another. When I was born, it was Russia. When I was going to school, it was Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe Luck? The city, what was it like?

speaker: interviewee
text: What was it like? It was a city surrounded with a river by name Svyr, S-V-Y-R, Svyr. And I was born by the bottom of the mountain, man-made mountain, which was a fortress in the medieval ages.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a large city?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I was born, it was 36,000. I mean, when I remember, when going to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there a large Jewish community there?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were 18,000. The whole city consisted of 36,000.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was a very large Jewish population?

speaker: interviewee
text: That time.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of work did people do there? Was there?

speaker: interviewee
text: All kinds manual work. My father-- my father was a construction-- a-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] a carpenter. And then he became in construction. And he was hired by the city, and state, and other government to build their [INAUDIBLE] places.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Lazer Lerner.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Chaia Leah Feldman.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did your mother do?

speaker: interviewee
text: A homemaker.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your home like?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were six of us. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The two were the last ones, we were twins, unidentical, opposite. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I was a tomboy. I was disobedient. And I got a lot of beating from my father, belts on me because I was not listening. But he loved me just the same. He loved me very much because I was very good in school. I was-- what would you call it-- gifted.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you describe your home? You had six sisters? What was--

speaker: interviewee
text: All then-- all of us were six, altogether.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the house like? Did you live in a house?

speaker: interviewee
text: We what?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you live in a house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We-- Father built the house. We lived in the back of the house. In the front, we had a tenant.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many rooms were there in the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a big kitchen, and a-- and a big room. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And a small room.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was Mom's day like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hard as could be. She would get up very early. She would have to milk the cow, except for Shabbat when she had a neighborly gentile to milk the cow. She was very orthodox, which I am not. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And she sewed our dresses. She would work maybe 16 hours. I don't know how many. She hardly had time to sleep, churned the butter, sewed dresses for us, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] do everything in the house.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you celebrate holidays at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very strict. She was orthodox. We didn't cook. We had a kind a type of an oven that you put Friday at noontime all that cholent, and whatnot, and kugel. And she lit. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And you would open noontime next day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you celebrate any of the other holidays, other than Shabbat?

speaker: interviewee
text: We-- in a way, we celebrated all of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of the special celebrations, Seder?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes, we had a Seder.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like?

speaker: interviewee
text: Father was sitting at the head of the table, and we were around the table. And we had wine and bracha. Not the wine, it was vodka, small, small portions.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did Mom cook special foods?

speaker: interviewee
text: They were very charitable people. My mother would bake starting Thursday. We had a big table in the entry before you went into the kitchen. And on that table was a lot of everything mother baked. And people would come around, and they would give it to them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. At first, I went to-- the closest was by the Russian church, a Ukrainaian school. They had only-- by the time I got there, they were having five grades only because Poland started to close up the schools. So when I came, they had five grades. Next year, they would have four grades, because then from the five Ukrainian school grades, I went to the Polish, to the sixth grade. It was hard on me because Polish was different than Ukrainian.

speaker: interviewer
text: What language was spoken in your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: With mother, Yiddish. With father, Russian.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then at sixth grade, you went to Polish school and had to learn Polish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Made my-- I remember my first mistakes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were those?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland-- in English, you don't know that. In Polish, for instance, [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] [SPEAKING POLISH], meaning, I have bread. I don't have bread. But you see, it changes because of the grammar. You don't have grammar here, so it's not a big deal.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what other schools did you go to?

speaker: interviewee
text: After sixth grade, in, in town, we had four high schools. The Russians had their own, all private. The Russians had their own high school. The Ukrainians had their own high school. And with that, every school had their own uniforms and berets. I was going-- I had no choice. I wouldn't be accepted to the government school. Was a no-no. So there was a private-- Jewish private school in Polish language. And that's where I went. After seventh grade, I went to the Jewish high school.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was-- the government school with a no-no because you were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: True.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that your first experience of antisemitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. There was more. We had in school these real antisemites. And what they do, they made the imitation, like it was one by name [? Mieska, ?] meaning a snake. And she was a snake. She said, you will say, [NON-ENGLISH] And you will say, [NON-ENGLISH] And together, it becomes like a mixture. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And that what it was. In elementary school, the, the principal-- it was a girls school. And the principal was very understanding. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] She wouldn't allow all this. And then we had to go. I belonged to the choir. The teacher was not discriminating. And when it came, the birthday day for the bishop, we went to sing for his birthday. And there, they had these young priests, clerics, they called them. And they were walking around with chocolate boxes and treats. And then the bishop started to [INAUDIBLE], to bless us. So everybody kneeled. And I wouldn't that time. I wouldn't kneel. So I and another girl were standing up.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Nothing. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then there is a Polish-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] every-- in Poland, every day has another saint's name, every day. There was a saint by name [INAUDIBLE] Holy Koska-- I don't know-- Koska something. And on this day, they took us to the city theater. We were to sing. We rehearsed together on Fridays afternoon with the boys who came from the boys school, to prepare. When we came to the theater, we sang our part. We went down to the audience. The teacher said, you wait here. And when it's finished, we'll go back to school. When I was sitting there, came a cleric and said, you leave. You don't belong here. I looked at him, and I didn't move. I stayed right there. I am a rebel in my lifetime. [LAUGHS] I ignored him, and I just kept sitting because the teacher said you go back to school. All along, it was a lot of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were most of your playmates Jewish, your friends?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were nine. You see, we went-- we were in the Ukrainian school. When we went to the Polish school, there were-- there were Czecho-- Czechish girl, Jewish girl. We were like-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] what I would call it-- black sheep. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] But we stand-- we did stand our ground, anyway. But we kept together, the group. Because the rest of them were different, not exactly accepted.

speaker: interviewer
text: That must have been difficult for you as a youngster.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, when you float in this atmosphere, you have to be just ignoring them or resist them.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then--

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't bring my pictures.

speaker: interviewer
text: Then you went to the Jewish school. That must have been a--

speaker: interviewee
text: To the private school.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was that like for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Terrible, because we paid-- let's call it dollars-- $60 a month. I were a-- I was a twin, so we had two of us. $60 for 12 months a year. It's only 10 months' schooling, but you pay for the summertime also. But then I don't know how they handled their finances. They-- this principal demanded a 13th month. And if you didn't bring the money, they would send you out unless you bring the money. So that's what we went through.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father worked very hard to bring up six girls and pay for school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Very much so.

speaker: interviewer
text: You and your sister were the youngest, you and your twin?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What were your other sisters doing at that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: The oldest-- depends what time of the year. She married when she was maybe 22 or 23. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And she had oldest three, I think-- two or three stillborn children, miscarriages or stillborn. Then she had the one. And I remember when-- you're not going to believe those years. A baby was maybe eight or nine months when he died. It came, and I didn't understand. It came a Jewish man who brought a black jacket, just about like you wear. And he wrapped that baby in it and took it like this and walked away with him. And I couldn't understand it. It hurt me so much. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then one of those was a living boy. When they started, the soviets first started to bomb our city, and we had a big call it field, or meadow, belonged to us. And they would cut the grass and put haystacks. And when they started the bombing, my oldest sister and her child, and a neighborly Russian woman hid in the haystack. And when they started to bomb, shrapnels fell and they tore off that clavicle of my sister. And she died there. The neighbor, it ripped off that part. On the way to the hospital, she died. And the boy, nothing happened to the boy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did the boy-- was taken care of by your parents?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They cried day and night as to what happened, I remember. Then when we were 14, we were going to high school, the two of us. And it broke out scarlet fever. My sister was the first to get it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your twin sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: And she was so sick. I remember when I woke up, my mother was walking and doing like this and crying. She was in, they called it, crisis. But she survived. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then when it broke on the scarlet fever, and the doctor said, now, they can be in one bed. That means I'm on this side of the bed, and you are in this side of the bed. You had my-- and I remember by then, we're allowed to eat. So mother made [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] cream wheat, cream of wheat. And she had her bowl, and I had mine. And I quickly ate up mine, and I grabbed for hers. And she wouldn't let me. So we all fell out of bed. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So--

speaker: interviewee
text: Then they allowed us to eat more. And then was easier. Those years, they didn't feed you. Now, they feed you, regardless.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you finished high school, and you graduated. And then what do you do?

speaker: interviewee
text: I took a course of nursing, like a Red Cross course in my home town. But that was hardly enough. And it was very, very-- atmosphere started to be war-like. People sensed the war. So they started to buy flour and things that they might need because everything disappeared from the market.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1938, I think, if I don't mislead you. Now, on that particular Friday, 1939, September the 1st-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] oh, wait a minute. I didn't tell you about the sanatorium for TB children. When the war was on, my sister and I, first on the west side of the city, I was working. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I didn't know what to do with myself. And I found myself-- my-- I'm confusing. My sister went to a clairvoyance woman. And she said, if you want, you have somebody that you're very worried about. If you want her to survive, she has to go south of Warsaw. If-- You have two choices. You can go to Grojec. It's a small town that time, only 5,000. And she could get through the war there. Or she could go further south to Kazimierz, a city. But I decided, I'll better go to the one that's closer to town. And I don't need to travel in the train when everybody recognize me. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] So I choose that one. And there, I didn't know what to do with myself. I thought I'll be a maid. And I went to a woman who was like referring you to households. I came there, and, and she wasn't there because her daughter was working by through the Germans in a field. And on the way back, she said, all the way in the top of the wagon filled with hay. And she reached out for a cherry. And she pulled the cherry, and she fell off and broke her spine. Therefore, the mother wasn't occupied what she used to do. She was going crazy running around town and crying, and lost her mind or something. In meantime, I need to be somewheres to sleep, And I was sitting all day waiting. Finally, the-- and there was a very poor, young couple, just married. And, and they had nothing. And I knocked on their door. And I said, go and check if she's back. Go and-- she was above them, living above them. And she didn't come. It was very late. And the next best, I knocked on the couple's door, and the put me between them in the bed for the night. They had only one bed.

speaker: interviewer
text: This-- the Germans had already occupied Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: The war was on. So this was in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in fact, when I was-- I think it was '38 yet.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I-- when I was to go home by train, I had to stay all night in the train waiting. And I was very hungry. Next to me was sitting a peasant woman. She had bread and milk, and she treated me. And then she gave me her address. And she said that I live in that place, there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And if you have no choice where to go, come, and I'll keep you. I was walking-- by the way, I forgot to tell you. When I was going by the train by myself, nobody was in the train because of curfew. I was the only in the whole train. Next to me was a package wrapped in a newspaper and tied with a string. I touched it. Nobody was there. I took it. I thought these are shoes. I thought these are man's shoes, in fact. When I came to my sister, my sister never went to ghetto. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] She was taken by a friend. Her boyfriend was a Polish Catholic doctor. And when she-- they were working in the same hospital. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] Her husband sent-- forgot now.

speaker: interviewer
text: If I can back up just a moment. Your sister went to a clairvoyant.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not my twin.

speaker: interviewer
text: Not your twin, one of your sisters. And then you went to Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I followed what she told me to do. Right, and this was in 1938. You were in Warsaw. What do you do in Warsaw? I was working on private cases.

speaker: interviewer
text: As a nurse.

speaker: interviewee
text: The doctor did refer me, were two doctors, two brother doctors. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I knew him, and he would refer me to places. I remember one time, I went to a case. And when I came, the patient was dead. And she was very huge in size. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I, nevertheless, had to stay there through the night. And in the morning, I was the one to wash her. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] And because she was too big, we cut her dress in the back, and we just pulled it down, all the way in the back, cut. That's how she was. Women, they buried them like this, in heels, or St. Mary's picture. That's how I placed that picture. I remember they gave me then to eat a sandwich with ham. And I don't eat these things to this day. So I threw-- I left it, and threw it in the basket and covered with paper.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so you stayed on as a nurse, working for this doctor in Warsaw?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened? You then--

speaker: interviewee
text: What happened to him? One day, the war was on, and I said, Dr.-- forgot his name now-- and he had also a brother who was a dermatologist. And I said to him, don't you see? We're going to perish, all of us. He said, you are young. You'll survive. He said, the war is like a-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] what do you call this?

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, while you think about it, we'll change the tape.
